criteria.criterion: Criterion A: ≥5 of 9 symptoms during the same 2-week period, representing a change from prior functioning. At least one symptom must be (1) depressed mood or (2) loss of interest or pleasure (anhedonia). Symptoms (each present nearly every day unless otherwise noted): 1. Depressed mood most of the day... 8. Diminished ability to think or concentrate, or indecisiveness. 9. Recurrent thoughts of death...
criteria.evidence: Patient reports 'feeling pretty sad and upset for the past four days' and 'inability to concentrate, particularly struggling with work tasks' but duration is only four days so far; no report of other symptoms like anhedonia, weight/appetite change, sleep issues, fatigue, guilt, or suicidal thoughts.

criteria.criterion: Criterion B: Functional impairment: the symptoms cause clinically significant distress or impairment in social, occupational, or other important areas of functioning.
criteria.evidence: Patient reports 'inability to concentrate, particularly struggling with work tasks for some reason'.

criteria.criterion: Criterion C: Substance/medical exclusion: the episode is not attributable to the physiological effects of a substance or another medical condition.
criteria.evidence: No patient report on substances or medical conditions; requires clinical assessment.

criteria.criterion: Criterion D: the episode is not better explained by schizoaffective disorder, schizophrenia, schizophreniform disorder, delusional disorder, or other specified/unspecified schizophrenia spectrum and psychotic disorders.
criteria.evidence: No patient report on psychotic symptoms or related disorders; requires clinical judgment.

criteria.criterion: Criterion E: there has never been a manic or hypomanic episode (unless these were substance-induced or attributable to a medical condition).
criteria.evidence: No patient report on history of mania/hypomania; requires clinical assessment.

criteria.criterion: Criterion A: Depressed mood for most of the day, for more days than not, as indicated by either subjective account or observation by others, for at least 2 years.
criteria.evidence: Patient reports symptoms 'for the past four days' only; 'Patient reports feeling pretty sad and upset for the past four days'.

criteria.criterion: Criterion B: Presence, while depressed, of two (or more) of the following: 1. Poor appetite or overeating. 2. Insomnia or hypersomnia. 3. Low energy or fatigue. 4. Low self-esteem. 5. Poor concentration or difficulty making decisions. 6. Feelings of hopelessness.
criteria.evidence: Patient reports 'inability to concentrate' (matches poor concentration) but only for four days; no reports on other symptoms or 2-year duration.

criteria.criterion: Criterion C: The symptoms cause clinically significant distress or impairment in social, occupational, or other important areas of functioning.
criteria.evidence: Patient reports 'inability to concentrate, particularly struggling with work tasks'.

criteria.criterion: Criterion D: The disturbance is not attributable to the physiological effects of a substance or another medical condition.
criteria.evidence: No patient report on substances or medical conditions; requires clinical assessment.

criteria.criterion: Criterion E: The disturbance is not better explained by a persistent schizoaffective disorder, schizophrenia, delusional disorder, or other specified or unspecified schizophrenia spectrum and other psychotic disorder.
criteria.evidence: No patient report on psychotic disorders; requires clinical judgment.

criteria.criterion: Criterion F: There has never been a manic episode and not better explained by cyclothymic disorder.
criteria.evidence: No patient report on manic history; requires clinical assessment.

criteria.criterion: Criterion A: The development of emotional or behavioral symptoms in response to an identifiable stressor(s) occurring within 3 months of the onset of the stressor(s).
criteria.evidence: Patient reports 'their dog died, which appears to be the trigger for feeling pretty sad and upset for the past four days' and symptoms started after the death.

criteria.criterion: Criterion B: These symptoms are clinically significant as evidenced by one or both of the following: 1. Marked distress that is out of proportion to the severity or intensity of the stressor. 2. Significant impairment in social or occupational (or other important areas) functioning.
criteria.evidence: Patient reports 'inability to concentrate, particularly struggling with work tasks' (possible impairment) but 'without knowing the reason why' and duration only four days; proportionality to dog death requires clinical judgment.

criteria.criterion: Criterion C: The stress-related disturbance does not meet the criteria for another mental disorder and is not merely an exacerbation of a preexisting mental disorder.
criteria.evidence: Patient reports symptoms for four days after dog death; cannot yet rule out other disorders like MDD; requires clinical judgment.

criteria.criterion: Criterion D: The symptoms do not represent normal bereavement.
criteria.evidence: Patient reports dog died as trigger; DSM-5 notes bereavement can be distinguished from disorder via clinical judgment.

criteria.criterion: Criterion E: Once the stressor or its consequences have terminated, the symptoms do not persist for more than an additional 6 months.
criteria.evidence: Symptoms ongoing for four days; dog death is recent; persistence after stressor termination not yet observable.
